title: Kirill Muller - duckplyr: Analyze large data wtih DuckDB and dplyr compatibility
description:
duckplyr: Analyze large data wtih DuckDB and dplyr compatibility (Kirill Muller)
Abstract: The duckplyr package is now stable; version 1.1.2 is on CRAN. It builds on top of DuckDB, a fast and flexible analytical engine that can work with larger-than-memory data from disk or cloud storage. All these features are available to duckplyr, with syntax and semantics much closer to R and the tidyverse. Use it to speed up existing code, to analyze Parquet or CSV files directly, or to access the plethora of exciting functionality provided by DuckDB.
Presented at the 2025 R/Pharma Conference Europe/US Track.
Resources mentioned in the presentation:
- Presentation materials GitHub repository: https://github.com/cynkra/r-in-pharma-2025/
oriTitle: Kirill Muller - duckplyr: Analyze large data wtih DuckDB and dplyr compatibility

title: Alanah Jones - Beyond Training: Evolving Strategies to Teach and Support R Adoption in Pharma
description:
Beyond Training: Evolving Strategies to Teach and Support R Adoption in Pharma (Alanah Jones)
Abstract: Teaching R effectively is just the starting point for lasting adoption in pharma. At GSK, we began by developing two core R courses written in bookdown and delivered interactively, an approach that enabled high completion rates.
Recognizing that training alone isn't enough, we expanded support through options like self-certification, intermediate courses, and a Resource Hub. We also launched AccelerateR to provide tailored support to early adopters during their transition.
Today, those efforts have scaled and evolved into Rburst, a cross-functional initiative designed not just to teach R, but to embed it into our ways of working across all of Biostatistics.
This talk will explore how our training strategy has matured into a broader support model, enabling wide-scale, integrated adoption of R in everyday work.
Presented at the 2025 R/Pharma Conference Europe/US Track.
oriTitle: Alanah Jones - Beyond Training: Evolving Strategies to Teach and Support R Adoption in Pharma

title: Pedro Silva - Validating Shiny Apps in Regulated Environments
description:
Validating Shiny Apps in Regulated Environments (Pedro Silva)
Abstract: Shiny apps are increasingly used to deliver interactive tools in clinical and healthcare settings. But when these tools are used in regulated environments, validation becomes essential. How can we ensure that our Shiny apps are trustworthy, without stifling innovation?
In this talk we’ll explore practical approaches to validating Shiny applications in regulated contexts, drawing on principles from software engineering, quality assurance, and risk based validation. We’ll discuss key challenges like traceability, documentation, and versioning, as well as share techniques for building apps that are easier to validate from the start.
I’ll highlight some of the tools and packages used in Jumping Rivers that can support validation workflows that satisfies both internal reviewers and external regulators, including the Litmusverse, a suite of R packages designed to assess code quality and generate validation evidence.
By the end of the session, you’ll understand: - Why validation is critical for Shiny apps in regulated contexts; - What elements make a Shiny app more or less validatable; - How to incorporate validation strategies into your development process.
This session is ideal for R users in pharma, clinical research, and healthcare who want to build confidence in their dashboards, while maintaining flexibility in how they work.
Presented at the 2025 R/Pharma Conference Europe/US Track.
Resources mentioned in the presentation:
- Presentation slides: https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1atV5rBLQF2vcCg2lraiutg_Xx2BOEklZzf9LJt842ig/edit?usp=sharing
oriTitle: Pedro Silva - Validating Shiny Apps in Regulated Environments

title: Daniel Sjoberg, Davide Garolini - How the {crane} Package Extends the Framework for Pharma Reporting
description:
Beyond {gtsummary}: How the {crane} Package Extends the Framework for Pharmaceutical Reporting (Daniel Sjoberg, Davide Garolini)
Abstract: {gtsummary} has become the most-used R package for clinical tables in the R ecosystem, winning awards from the American Statistical Association and Posit. Building on this foundation, we created {crane}, an open-source extension that facilitates reporting requirements in the pharmaceutical space. Because {crane} and {gtsummary} are built upon Analysis Results Datasets (ARDs), study teams can take advantage of this foundation that streamlines the QC process and allows for simple re-use of calculated results in subsequent reporting.
In this session we show how: (1) a vanilla {gtsummary} script instantly upgrades when {crane} is loaded; (2) ARD-based outputs make QC a straightforward operation; (3) LLMs can summarise the language-agnostic ARD results for medical writers in seconds. You’ll leave with a blueprint of how to adapt {crane} for your reporting needs or (or roll your own) and shorten table turnaround on day one.
Presented at the 2025 R/Pharma Conference Europe/US Track.
Resources mentioned in the presentation:
- Presentation slides: https://www.danieldsjoberg.com/RinPharma-crane-2025/
oriTitle: Daniel Sjoberg, Davide Garolini - How the {crane} Package Extends the Framework for Pharma Reporting

title: Sven-Eric Schelhorn - {llumen}: An agentic LLM framework for documents, databases, foundation models
description:
{llumen}: An agentic LLM framework for biomedical documents, databases & foundation models (Sven-Eric Schelhorn)
Abstract: We introduce {llumen}, an R package used at Merck KGaA, Darmstadt, Germany that implements an agentic framework bases on {ellmer} and enables pharmaceutical researchers to work with biomedical documents, large real-world evidence and biomarker databases, as well as with biological foundation models using large language models as orchestrators. {llumen} currently supports the following functionalities: **Large language models** - Runs on standard company laptops (Windows, Linux, or macOS). - Allows using a wide range of private (Azure), public (OpenAI, Anthropic, Bedrock), and local (llama.cpp) LLMs. - Provides both programmatic and fully interactive (chatbot) ways to interact with the LLM. - Supports chain-of-thoughts, tree-of-thoughts, and ReAct reasoning strategies. - Supports context management to maintain conversation history and state. 
Text analysis
- Supports embeddings (Azure, public, or local) using a local, high-performance vector DB (based on {ragnar}).
- Implements chunking office documents (Word, Excel, Powerpoint, RTF, PDF) into the vector DB.
- While reading office documents, supports extracting tables with summary data.
- Enables retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) using the vector DB, either explicitly or by using a RAG tool that the LLM calls.
- Emulates the Future House's PaperQA2 approach for summarizing and re-ranking parts of semantically related documents so that answer quality and comprehensiveness is significantly boosted.
- Extracts text from documents to fill structured templates, such as the JSON-based {llumen} Analysis Results Schema (LARS). Extracted JSONs are directly validated by the LLM using an R tool.
- Supports automatic diagram generation from texts, for instance to capture the main concepts of a paper in two or three flow charts.
- Provides advanced text preprocessing and cleaning capabilities for various document types.
- Offers customizable tokenization and text segmentation methods.
Agentic tool use and codegen
- Allows the LLM to search the internet via Google and access the textual content of websites in a controlled manner. Also includes specialized query tools for Pubmed, Pubchem, and Wikipedia.
- Enables the LLM agent to query Parquet files, Neo4j knowledge graphs, and GraphQL APIs using SQL, Cypher and GraphQL codegen in a secure context. The database agents are very capable and can answer complex, open-ended scientific questions using these data sources.
- Enables the LLM agent to use local foundation models, such as Google's TxGemma model for pharmaceutical applications, for instance for predicting solubility of small molecules.
- Uses multimodal LLMs to accurately diagnose cancer histopathology images with very comprehensive pathology reports that investigate architectural and cytological Features of H&E slides.
- Makes it easy to give the LLM new R tool capabilities (basically all R functions), share tools via MCP (built-in pure R MCP server), or utilize tools of external MCP server as internal tools (via a MCP tool wrapper).
- Supports dynamic tool creation and registration during runtime.
- Provides a framework for defining custom tools and integrating them seamlessly with the LLM.
Presented at the 2025 R/Pharma Conference Europe/US Track.
Resources mentioned in the presentation:
- Presentation slides: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1g0_O_wSoeBHzeXl6DF3-NIY7gT2Bk62_/view?usp=sharing
oriTitle: Sven-Eric Schelhorn - {llumen}: An agentic LLM framework for documents, databases, foundation models

title: John Coene - Build Model Context Protocol servers and clients in R
description:
Build Model Context Protocol servers and clients in R (John Coene)
Abstract: The mcpr package provides a comprehensive R implementation of the Model Context Protocol (MCP), a standardized JSON-RPC 2.0 interface that enables R applications to expose computational capabilities to AI models. This package bridges the gap between R's statistical computing environment and modern AI assistants by allowing R developers to create MCP servers that expose tools, resources, and prompts as well as client functionality to interact with existing MCP servers.
Key features include schema-based tool definitions, multi-modal response support, and seamless integration with popular AI development environments including Claude Code, Cursor, and VS Code. This implementation democratizes AI-R integration by providing a standards-based approach to exposing R's extensive ecosystem to conversational AI interfaces, opening new possibilities for interactive data analysis and statistical computing workflows.
Presented at the 2025 R/Pharma Conference Europe/US Track.
Resources mentioned in the presentation:
- {mcpr}: Model Context Protocol for R https://mcpr.opifex.org/
- Presentation slides: https://rrr.is/mcpr
oriTitle: John Coene - Build Model Context Protocol servers and clients in R

title: Conor Moloney - Mosaic: Open-Source, ARS-Driven Automation of Standard TFLs
description:
Mosaic: Open-Source, ARS-Driven Automation of Standard TFLs (Conor Moloney)
Abstract: CDISC standards help streamline datasets (e.g. SDTM and ADaM), yet analysis results lack a common model. The emerging Analysis Results Standard (ARS) will help to fill this gap with machine-readable specifications. Mosaic couples ARS with an open-source stack to generate fully validated standard TFLs, fast and reproducibly.
How it works
Mosaic captures the Analysis Results Dataset (ARD) requirement in ARS-aligned YAML; updates and changes are made by editing this metadata, not code. After LinkML validation, a Python layer stores the YAML in a database via a SQL Alchemy ORM engine. The results-generation layer is intentionally language-agnostic . The rules that transform ADaM datasets into the ARD are expressed as metadata that can be rendered in any statistical language. Mosaic’s implementation uses R to derive the ARD.
A React UI reads the validated ARD. Here, users have controlled customisation options. Users can preview each TFL shell in real time and export regulator-ready RTF files suitable for submission packages.
Mosaic replaces ad-hoc programming with a transparent, standards-based pipeline, accelerating TFL delivery while safeguarding traceability and regulatory compliance. This reallocates programmer effort from repetitive coding to high-value scientific review
Presented at the 2025 R/Pharma Conference Europe/US Track.
oriTitle: Conor Moloney - Mosaic: Open-Source, ARS-Driven Automation of Standard TFLs

title: Jenny Dusendang, Sundeep Bath - Integrating Collab Programming with Traceability and Reproducibility
description:
Integrating Collaborative Programming with Automated Traceability and Reproducibility in Pharma (Jenny Dusendang, Sundeep Bath)
Abstract: To enhance integrity of research and study findings, data scientists should ensure that studies are traceable and reproducible, which involves meticulous management of datasets, tracking code changes, and robust storage of results.
Without infrastructure to support reproducibility efforts, documentation, dependency management, and version control processes can be manual, unreliable, and unclear. This creates problems with determining when analysis changes occurred, which version of study results were produced by which version of code, and whether all study steps are processed in proper order and appropriately
documented.
Implementing procedures and technical infrastructure helps to maintain and automate reproducibility and traceability. To ensure that code can be executed consistently across multiple compute environments, we structure analysis scripts into parameterized pipelines within an isolated Docker container environment which specifies all versions and dependencies. We integrate Continuous Integration (CI) and Continuous Delivery (CD) into analysis pipelines to enable automatic rerunning of analyses following code modifications and storage of results in the cloud. Our process integrates and improves collaborative programming by providing code reviewers with the validated outputs that are produced by the code. By design, study close-out and compliance activities are incorporated within our infrastructure.
In this paper we will discuss how we implemented and adapted DevOps best-practices like CI/CD in a collaborative coding environment to work for epidemiological studies and real-world data projects. Although the concepts discussed are applicable to many tools, our implementation uses Git, GitHub Actions, SQL, Python, R, Docker, and AWS S3. This content is applicable for all skill levels.
https://www.lexjansen.com/pharmasug/2025/OS/PharmaSUG-2025-OS-111.pdf
Presented at the 2025 R/Pharma Conference Europe/US Track.
Resources mentioned in the presentation:
- Template GitHub repository: https://github.com/graticule-life/PharmaSUG-DevOps-for-Pharma
oriTitle: Jenny Dusendang, Sundeep Bath - Integrating Collab Programming with Traceability and Reproducibility

title: Max Kuhn - TabPFN: A Deep-Learning Solution for Tabular Data
description:
TabPFN: A Deep-Learning Solution for Tabular Data (Max Kuhn)
Abstract: There have been numerous proposals for deep neural networks for tabular data, such as rectangular data sets (e.g., data frames). To date, none have really worked well and take far too long to train. TabPFN is a model that emulates a Bayesian approach and trains a deep learning model on a prior of simulated tabular datasets. Version 2 was released this year and offers several significant advantages, but also has one notable disadvantage. I'll introduce this model and show an example.
Presented at the 2025 R/Pharma Conference Europe/US Track.
Resources mentioned in the presentation:
- Presentation slides: https://topepo.github.io/2025-r-pharma/
oriTitle: Max Kuhn - TabPFN: A Deep-Learning Solution for Tabular Data

title: Building the Future of Data Apps: LLMs Meet Shiny
description:
GenAI in Pharma 2025 kicks off with Posit's Phil Bowsher and Garrick Aiden-Buie sharing a technical overview of how LLMs can integrate with Shiny applications and much more!
Abstract: When we think of LLMs (large language models), usually what comes to mind are general purpose chatbots like ChatGPT or code assistants like GitHub Copilot. But as useful as ChatGPT and Copilot are, LLMs have so much more to offer—if you know how to code. In this demo Garrick will explain LLM APIs from zero, and have you building and deploying custom LLM-empowered data workflows and apps in no time.
Resources mentioned in the session:
* GitHub Repository for session: https://github.com/gadenbuie/genAI-2025-llms-meet-shiny
* {mcptools} - Model Context Protocols servers and clients https://posit-dev.github.io/mcptools/
* {vitals} - Large language model evaluation for R https://vitals.tidyverse.org/
oriTitle: Building the Future of Data Apps: LLMs Meet Shiny

title: Interactive and Instant: GenAI-driven Creation of Clinical Trial Applications
description:
Interactive and Instant: GenAI-driven Creation of Clinical Trial Applications - Olga Mierzwa-Sulima & Jakub Nowicki (Appsilon)
Abstract: Building exploratory analysis dashboards for clinical trials traditionally requires considerable expertise, extensive time, and deep familiarity with specialized frameworks such as R/Shiny and {teal}. In this talk, we share our experience exploring how Generative AI can significantly streamline this process. We will present a tool we developed, powered by Claude Code, that enables biostatisticians and clinical researchers to effortlessly create and immediately preview R/Shiny applications tailored for exploratory clinical data analysis using {teal}.
Throughout our journey, we experimented with various GenAI-driven solutions, including code editors integrated with AI assistance tools (e.g., GitHub Copilot), and approaches leveraging MCP servers. Our current implementation is an interactive AI-powered chat interface designed to simplify the application-building experience, providing users with immediate application previews, and reducing both development complexity and the learning curve associated with the {teal} package.
We will demonstrate how GenAI-driven solutions can empower users with limited programming backgrounds to rapidly build effective exploratory analysis dashboards, discuss challenges encountered, and highlight insights gained through our iterative development process.
Resources mentioned in the presentation:
* Teal - Interactive exploratory data analysis with Shiny web-applications https://insightsengineering.github.io/teal/latest-tag/
oriTitle: Interactive and Instant: GenAI-driven Creation of Clinical Trial Applications

title: Building an AI Pair Programmer for ADaM - Ari Knoph & Anthony Rajecki-Doyle
description:
Building an AI Pair Programmer for ADaM - Ari Knoph & Anthony Rajecki-Doyle (Novo Nordisk)
Abstract: At Novo Nordisk, we seek to deliver medicines more effectively and efficiently by leveraging the capabilities of Generative AI to accelerate our processes.
We want to support our clinical trial programming activities with AI tools and coding assistants, scaling the output and impact of our statistical programmers.
Delivering a product in this space poses numerous scientific and technical challenges:
What is the right context to expose to an externally hosted model?
What workflow will best support our employees and leverage their domain knowledge?
What design choices will best integrate with our existing IT landscape?
In this presentation we will discuss the features we built and experimentation conducted to tackle the above questions, and how we are integrating Generative AI into the ADaM programming processes.
The work presented represents a joint venture between our Biometrics and AI & Analytices organization and in collaboration with Microsoft’s Industry Solutions Engineering team.
Resources mentioned in the presentation:
* GitHub Copilot - https://github.com/features/copilot
* Semantic Kernel - https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/semantic-kernel/overview/
oriTitle: Building an AI Pair Programmer for ADaM - Ari Knoph & Anthony Rajecki-Doyle

title: Enabling GenAI Capabilities for Statistical Programmers - Sam Parmar
description:
Enabling GenAI Capabilities for Statistical Programmers - Sam Parmar (Pfizer)
Abstract: At Pfizer, we are committed to enhancing the capabilities of our statistical programmers by leveraging GenAI. Our goal is to empower R users with AI tools to streamline workflows and boost productivity. We have integrated GitHub Copilot within the RStudio IDE, resulting in significant time savings and improved documentation. Additionally, we introduced the voxr package, which simplifies interaction with our internal Vox LLM API, enabling tasks such as text generation and sentiment analysis using state-of-the-art language models. This presentation will showcase the features we’ve developed, and the experimentation conducted to enhance productivity and LLM model interaction across the organization. We have observed significant time savings driven by improved debugging, documentation, and custom training. This presentation will discuss the features we have built, and the development and testing conducted to address key challenges, such as integration with external open-source efforts, ensuring responsible use, and improving performance by providing the right context. By expanding access to these tools and providing custom training, we aim to create a more empowered and productive user base.

title: Maximizing LLM Potential: How Data Structure and Context Drive Quality Insights - Emily Yates
description:
Maximizing LLM Potential: How Data Structure and Context Drive Quality Insights - Emily Yates (Formation Bio)
Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) promise to revolutionize clinical data analysis but struggle with messy, imperfect datasets common in drug development research. This presentation demonstrates how the inclusion of comprehensive context dramatically improves LLM output quality in clinical applications without extensive data cleaning. By enriching models with data context (field-level metadata, data dictionaries, relational mappings) as well as use case context (semantic data mapping, study design, domain ontologies), we enable flexible natural language exploration while maintaining quality and consistency. This approach bridges the gap between imperfect human queries and precise answers needed for pharmaceutical decision-making, revealing insights within complex, real-world datasets.
Resources mentioned in the presentation:
* {shinychat} - Bring your Shiny apps to life with LLM-powered chat interfaces https://posit-dev.github.io/shinychat/
* {chatlas} - Your friendly guide to building LLM chat apps in Python with less effort and more clarity https://posit-dev.github.io/chatlas/
* {ellmer} - Chat with Large Language models https://ellmer.tidyverse.org/
* Claude Code - https://claude.com/product/claude-code
oriTitle: Maximizing LLM Potential: How Data Structure and Context Drive Quality Insights - Emily Yates

title: Agents and MCP and RAG, Oh My! - Devin Pastoor
description:
Agents and MCP and RAG,  Oh My! - Devin Pastoor (A2-Ai)
Abstract: The GenAI landscape has rapidly evolved over the last year in how information can be aggregated and acted upon. Particularly dramatic is the shift to “Agentic” workflows that allow work to be done on behalf of the user or enable LLM applications themselves to do increasingly sophisticated multi-stage actions such as research and code generation and review. This talk will work through our journey and perspectives learned around a shift away from isolated chat based tools (eg chatbots) to more integrated tooling and how we are working to optimize value and efficiency for end users while still maintaining governance and oversight over usage.
oriTitle: Agents and MCP and RAG, Oh My! - Devin Pastoor

title: AIR: AI Assistant for R Programming - Vincent Shen
description:
AIR: AI Assistant for R Programming - Vincent Shen (Roche)
Abstract: We will present a modular, multi-agent coding assistant designed to streamline and enhance the coding experience for clinical insights generation using R packages. Built on a collaborative multi-agent architecture, the system is composed of specialized agents that handle distinct tasks. It intelligently blends publicly available resources with Roche’s internal process knowledge to deliver context-aware code suggestions. An embedded webR environment enables rapid, in-app execution for immediate feedback and iteration. We are currently exploring integration via MCPs to extend the assistant's utility into IDEs such as VSCode and Positron, ensuring seamless support across the developer workflow.
Resources mentioned in the presentation:
* WebR - R in the browser https://docs.r-wasm.org/webr/latest/
* FastAPI - https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/
oriTitle: AIR: AI Assistant for R Programming - Vincent Shen

title: Inside an AI Project: From Idea to Value - Jay Timmerman
description:
Inside an AI Project: From Idea to Value - Jay Timmerman (Biogen)
Abstract: Turning an AI idea into real business value isn’t easy. This talk breaks down the key steps in an AI project, how to define its impact, and common challenges—with tips on how to overcome them.
oriTitle: Inside an AI Project: From Idea to Value - Jay Timmerman

title: Natural Language to Code: AI-Powered Automation for CDISC Compliant Datasets - Vina Ro & Ian Sturdy
description:
Natural Language to Code: AI-Powered Automation for CDISC-Compliant Datasets - Vina Ro & Ian Sturdy (Eli Lilly)
Abstract: This talk introduces a generative AI-powered system that converts natural language coding specifications into R programs used to generate CDISC-compliant datasets, significantly reducing development time while ensuring compliance and accuracy. Leveraging techniques such as prompt engineering and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), the session will delve into the system’s technical architecture, key lessons learned, and its potential to transform clinical data programming.
Resources mentioned in the presentation:
* {sdtm.oak} - SDTM data transformation engine https://pharmaverse.github.io/sdtm.oak/
* {admiral} - ADaM in R Asset Library https://pharmaverse.github.io/admiral/cran-release/
* {cards} - CDISC Analysis Results Data https://insightsengineering.github.io/cards/latest-tag/
oriTitle: Natural Language to Code: AI-Powered Automation for CDISC Compliant Datasets - Vina Ro & Ian Sturdy

title: Considerations of LLM's Benefits and Risks - Jake Gagnon
description:
Considerations of LLM's Benefits and Risks - Phoebe Jiang, Jake Gagnon, Cedric Liu, Lira Pi (Biogen)
Abstract: LLMs in recent years have become immensely popular due to their ability to generate and understand text, images, audio, and video. In this talk, we will discuss the advantages of LLMs while also being mindful of their limitations and risks.
oriTitle: Considerations of LLM's Benefits and Risks - Jake Gagnon

description:
Positron Assistant for Developing Shiny Apps - Tom Mock (Posit)
Abstract: This talk will explore building AI Apps with a focus on Positron Assistant for Shiny developer experience and in-IDE tooling for accelerating app creation. This talk will discuss tools like ellmer / chatlas / querychat / shinychat and compare it to Positron Assistant.
Resources mentioned in the presentation:

oriTitle: Positron Assistant for Developing Shiny Apps - Tom Mock

title: Using Git for a multilingual approach to trial activities
description:
Using Git for a multilingual approach to trial activities - Nicolaj Thostrup

Abstract: This presentation explores the innovative use of Git to facilitate a multilingual approach in clinical trial execution at Novo Nordisk. It highlights the transition from SAS to R, driven by the need for improved efficiency and code reusability. The presentation details the setup of a new version control system, statistical computing environment, and the integration of Git and CI pipelines to manage and execute programs in multiple languages. It emphasizes the benefits of Git, including enhanced traceability, new review processes, and intelligent branch management. The future vision includes seamless orchestration of programs, independent of language and manual execution, ensuring a unified and efficient workflow.

Presented at the 2024 R/Pharma Conference
author: Nicolaj Thostrup

title: Implementation of {teal} Shiny apps in DMC activity
description:
Implementation of {teal} Shiny apps in DMC activity - Peng Zhang

Abstract: There have been two pilot studies discussing submission to the FDA through R Shiny. With such applications, an efficient and interactive review can now be conducted. While most of the applications focus on using R Shiny applications within an organization for internal review of compounds, R Shiny packages such as {teal} can be implemented for DMC activities. Interactive dashboards using R to perform analysis, which can be inclusive to study-specified analysis and endpoint, such as using {rtables}, {tern}, and {teal}. Moreover, such an approach can provide downloadable/portable files for further reference. In this session, we will discuss the feasibility of such an application into DMC activity.

Resources mentioned in the talk:
- R Consortium Submissions Pilot 2 (Shiny application) https://github.com/RConsortium/submissions-pilot2
- R Consortium Submissions Pilot 4 (Shiny application powered by webR) https://github.com/RConsortium/submissions-pilot4-webR

author: Peng Zhang

title: Gearing our industry Statisticians up for Software success : A {phase1b} journey
description:
Gearing our industry Statisticians up for Software success : A {phase1b} journey - Audrey Yeo

Abstract: Trial statisticians write software and are well placed to contribute to analytical solutions that inform business and clinical decision making. Early conversations about incorporating software engineering competence in a trials statistician’s (Sabanés Bové, 2023) already impressive toolbox have started to emerge with the use of the common language of R. I share my personal experience about the actions and attitudes to achieve a state-of-art solution called phase1b and highlight good software engineering principles and ways of working. The package phase1b (Yeo et al, 2024)  is a flexible toolkit that evaluates efficacy and futility analysis within the Bayesian framework for early Oncology trials. The R package informs decision making on whether the drug of concern warrants further investment. Since the evaluation of the efficacy and futility impacts business and clinical decisions in pharma, it is advantageous that the industry strives to understand and create the conditions for more Software success such as the phase1b.

Resources mentioned in the talk:
- {phase1b} Calculations for decisions on Phase 1B trials https://genentech.github.io/phase1b/main/

title: Building living apps in Shiny for better modeling insights and outcomes
description:
Building living apps in Shiny for better modeling insights and outcomes - Benjamin Bucior

Abstract: Shiny is not just a tool for rapidly exploring and visualizing data. It can also be leveraged as a highly effective framework for interacting with models. In this talk, we'll explore how Shiny can be used to customize simulations and run them dynamically. By providing stakeholders more control over model decisions, data scientists can develop deeper partnerships and engagement. This approach helps to build a common language, share domain knowledge, and ultimately find practical solutions for the challenges at hand.

Presented at the 2024 R/Pharma Conference
author: Benjamin Bucior

title: The R Shiny App TNfinder for tumor-normal expression analysis
description:
The R Shiny App TNfinder for tumor-normal expression analysis - Chengcheng Liu

Abstract: We developed a Shiny application to deliver fast and flexible tumor-normal gene expression analysis based on TCGA and GTEx data. Compared to the widely-used public tool GEPIA, TNfinder offers more tailored functions including gene signature analysis, T-N cutoff evaluation, population estimation, tumor-specific marker detection, and enhanced visualization options. In clinical programming, ensuring the quality and compliance of deliverables is paramount to uphold regulatory standards and safeguard patient safety. The Shiny application is effectively used for creating interactive web application that facilitate quality compliance checks in clinical programming frameworks.

title: Transforming EDC Data into Practical Insights: The Power of an One-Click R-Shiny App
description:
Transforming EDC Data into Practical Insights: The Power of an One-Click R-Shiny App - Keting LV

Abstract: In our daily study practice, we identified the need for a concise and straightforward tool to help study teams extract significant information from the latest EDC raw data. However, study teams often find themselves overwhelmed by the vast amount of EDC data and overly complex data review tools. To address this, we developed a one-click R-Shiny App that bridges EDC data directly to actionable outputs, enabling the detection of critical signals related to data quality.
The goal of this one-click R-Shiny App is to resolve the following challenges:

1. Provide a global dashboard that accommodates all company-level studies.
2. Detect patterns between the overall population and the China subgroup in safety data, end-of-treatment, end-of-study, protocol deviations, and biomarker sample collection.
3. Extract the most critical exception listings.
4. Address the issue of variable names lacking direct access to variable labels.

title: {BayesCVI} A Bayesian cluster validity index
description:
{BayesCVI} A Bayesian cluster validity index with medical applications - Onthada Preedasawakul, Nathakhun Wiroonsri, Frank Yang

Abstract: The Bayesian Cluster Validity Index (BCVI) is a new concept that directly applies the Bayesian framework to CVIs. This connection is unique and has not been explored before in the literature. In this talk, we introduce BCVI, which can be used in conjunction with any existing CVIs, particularly advantageous for users who have prior knowledge of the expected number of clusters in their datasets. We will explore the BCVI concept in depth, demonstrating its integration with the BayesCVI available on CRAN and applying it to diverse real-world applications such as health science data and MRI image brain tumor detection.

title: Breaking down cognitive barriers for new members to your project and your future self
description:
Breaking down cognitive barriers for new members to your project and your future self - Vedha Viyash

Abstract: At Appsilon, we focus on helping diverse teams build Shiny apps to solve their problems. Over time, we've observed common project patterns that hinder onboarding and create barriers to productive contributions. In this discussion, we will detail these patterns and provide strategies to mitigate them, supported by case studies of projects that have successfully overcome these challenges. These barriers include a lack of documentation, complex setup processes, insufficient dependency management, poor code organization, and inadequate testing, among others. At first, these topics may seem more closely related to the responsibilities of professional developers rather than members working within business lines. However, it becomes much easier to address these issues with access to some helpful tools. We will discuss these tools and how to implement them in your project with minimal effort. Defining such standards will ensure that future projects and teams work seamlessly and effectively.

title: Hung Pan - R, Docker and Nextflow - Building the stable Data pipeline
description:
R, Docker and Nextflow - Building the stable Data pipeline - Wei-Hung Pan

Abstract: Stability of statistical results is essential in drug development. Many research groups develop in-house pipelines for data processing and analysis, incorporating with R packages, internal computations, and even integrate with other language like Python. However, untracked tool updates can lead to result variability or pipeline failures.

At Silence Therapeutics, we've implemented a three-tiered approach to ensure result reliability, reproducibility, and robustness:

1. Automated semantic versioning: Tracks changes in our data pipeline when internal or external developers update functions.
2. Docker containerization: Ensures consistent environments for reproducible results.
3. Nextflow integration: Enables scalable execution of our containerized pipelines on AWS cloud.

This comprehensive system provides a solid foundation for our drug development efforts by maintaining pipeline integrity and result consistency.

title: Building a reproducible workflow using {targets}
description:
Building a reproducible workflow using {targets} - Masahiko Takemura, Kensuke Sasaki

Abstract: Reproducibility and efficiency are critical in data analysis workflows, particularly in complex projects with multiple interdependent tasks. The {targets} package in R offers a robust solution for orchestrating reproducible and scalable workflows. By defining and managing a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of tasks, {targets} ensures that each step in the workflow is executed in the correct order and only when necessary, saving time and computational resources. In this presentation, we will demonstrate how to build a reproducible workflow using {targets}. We will explore the core features of the package, including target definition, dependency management, and automated pipeline execution. Through practical examples, we will show how {targets} can be integrated with other R packages to handle data processing, modeling, and visualization seamlessly. Additionally, we will discuss practical tips for maintaining workflows, ensuring they remain efficient and manageable as project complexity grows. Whether you are new to {targets} or looking to enhance your workflow automation, this session will provide valuable insights and practical techniques to elevate your data analysis projects.

title: Clinical Report & Visualization Ensemble (carver)
description:
Clinical Analysis Report and Visualization Ensemble in R (carver) - Smriti Anand, Aravind Reddy, Smitha Ankinakatte

Abstract: Very few industry-wide standards exist for clinical reporting, but open-source technologies are bridging this gap. This presentation introduces our R package and Shiny application, Carver (Clinical Analysis Report and Visualization Ensemble in R), which generates clinical submission-ready tables and graphs. An earlier version focusing on Adverse Events related reports was developed by our team in collaboration with Phuse, ASA and FDA. It is now expanded as an open-source Pfizer project, under spotlight of CDISC Open Source Alliance (COSA). This project references FDA Integrated TLF Guide, CDISC Guidelines and CDISC Standard Data.

{carver} uses standardized functions to build multiple clinical reports – under domains like Adverse Events, Lab Findings and Demography in Therapeutic Areas like Vaccine and Oncology. In addition to templates to create static tables and figures, the modularized Shiny application can also be used to generate interactive plots – with a feature to drill down to patient-level analysis. {carver} uses packages from the tidyverse suite, plotly, ggsurvfit, shiny, shinydashboard, shinyBS etc. For tables, we built a wrapper around Flextable package to dynamically fit a submission-ready format. The R functions are unit-tested, use tidy principles and CI/CD workflows were implemented.

We aim for {carver} to be used for regulatory submissions and for clinical review. Another potential outcome could be to embed study data within a Shiny Dashboard and include it in clinical submission-package. We are seeking collaborators to expand its reporting capabilities across the pharma industry.

title: Automation of reporting statistical results with R
description:
Automation of reporting statistical results with R (mocktables, summary tables, result narrations) - Smitha Ankinakatte

Abstract: For one who works with programming, working on word documents such as creating tables, adjusting spaces, fonts seems to be tiring. In the pharma domain, statisticians are involved in one such task of building mock shells for tables and replicating the same table structure (using programming software) to report statistical results. Statisticians are also required to provide statistical narratives for key results, which involves copying numerical values from summary tables and manually crafting the narrative - another time-consuming job. Many pharmaceutical companies have developed their own R libraries, some of which are available in the public domain. However, the output formats (i.e., the class of output objects) from these libraries are often not flexible and difficult to adapt to specific end-user requirements.

This abstract proposes a solution to these challenges using R by:
1. Creating Custom R Functions (Macros) for table layout: We propose building R functions that can generate table mock shells, which can later be reused to report statistical outputs. For example, a function like `fun_demo_tab` could be used to create a mock table for demographic data to be included in a TLF (Tables, Listings, and Figures) mock shell. The same function could then be reused to present the statistical summary of demographic data in the final report.

2. Automating Statistical Narration: We suggest developing an R macro along with RMarkdown that can automatically extract numerical values from summary tables and integrate them into a predefined narrative framework. This would significantly reduce the time statisticians spend on this task, cutting it down from hours to just a few seconds.

Resources mentioned in the talk:
- {tidyverse} R packages for data science https://www.tidyverse.org/
- {officer} Manipulation of Microsoft Word and PowerPoint Documents https://davidgohel.github.io/officer/

title: Page - An arrow towards the future: A critical look at data formats
description:
An arrow towards the future: A critical look at data formats for clinical reporting - Craig Gower-Page

Abstract: Whilst the number of multilingual programming teams for clinical study work has been growing, so has the issue of cross-language data compatibility. If care is not taken we risk antiquated or inadequate formats becoming the de facto standard, or worse yet a return to complete interoperability from the use of language specific data formats (Rds, Pickle, etc). This presentation will discuss a number of open data formats, with a comparative analysis of their strengths and weaknesses. In particular, this presentation will introduce and recommend Apache Parquet, a high performance binary data format that comes with client libraries for most popular analytical programming languages as well as integration into many popular tools and databases. Finally, the talk will touch on our experiences and challenges of trying to integrate Apache Parquet within our data analysis workflows at Roche.

title: Teal Builder - The Fastest Way to Build Reproducible Teal Apps
description:
Teal Builder - The Fastest Way to Build Reproducible Teal Apps - Freeman Wang, Andrew McNeil 

Abstract: This session will introduce Teal Builder, a powerful Shiny application designed to simplify the creation of Teal apps without requiring users to write any R code. The presentation will cover the purpose and functionality of Teal Builder, demonstrate how to run the app locally, and provide a live demonstration of its capabilities. Teal Builder not only streamlines the process of building Teal apps but also provides a platform for generalizing certain internal statistical methods and processes into reusable modules to be used by more studies.

Teal Builder offers a user-friendly interface that guides users through data selection from multiple sources, module selection and compatibility validation based on the selected data. The app generates reproducible R code for running the Teal app or deploying it to Posit Connect, making the process efficient and accessible. It fulfills the unmet need of having an interactive data display for all studies, promoting Teal and R/Shiny adoption and usage to new R and non-R users.

Teal Builder comprises three R packages: teal.builder, teal.builder.modules, and teal.builder.checks. The teal.builder package contains functions for running the Shiny app and serves as the entry point for most users. The teal.builder.modules package offers a collection of predefined module specifications, including module names, descriptions, data requirements, and reproducible code. The teal.builder.checks package provides functions to ensure user data adheres to specifications, offering detailed feedback when necessary.

During the demonstration, we will walk through the user journey of building a Teal app with Teal Builder. We'll showcase how a complex app for exploring ADaM datasets, which typically requires around 500 lines of code, can be generated in just a few minutes. This session will highlight the efficiency and user-friendliness of Teal Builder, making it an invaluable tool for those working with Teal apps.

title: Building rome in a month: How open-source technologies empower small pharma
description:
Building rome in a month: How open-source technologies empower small pharma - Chendi Liao

Abstract:Traditionally, small pharmaceutical or biotech companies, often limited by resources, tend to rely on FSPs or CROs for statistical programming and analytics support. The emergence and maturation of enterprise-caliber open-source software and tools present new and transformative opportunities for these organizations by helping to enhance data accessibility, streamline analysis workflows, and foster collaborative innovation. However, building these types of infrastructure within small pharma involves navigating budget constraints, limited IT resources, stakeholder buy-in and the need for specialized expertise. Overcoming these challenges requires strategic planning, prioritization, and training. This presentation will delve into the practical aspects and lessons learned from our experience of building up open-source technologies, such as the Posit computing environment, from scratch within the Clinical Development department at Recursion. By tackling these infrastructural hurdles, the integration of open-source toolsets has significantly improved our data analysis capabilities, accelerating drug development and positioning our company at the forefront of pharmaceutical innovation.

title: Piloting into the Future: Publicly Available R-based Submissions to FDA
description:
Piloting into the Future: Publicly Available R-based Submissions to the FDA - Ben Straub, Joel Laxamana

Abstract: Recent years have seen a significant shift towards the adoption of open-source software like R by statisticians and analysts in both industry and regulatory agencies. This trend offers immense benefits, including cutting-edge statistical tools, interactive dashboard packages like Shiny, and adaptability to modern data science trends. Despite these advantages, the pharmaceutical industry's broader adoption of R for drug submissions has been limited. To address this, the R Consortium R Submissions Working Group (RCRSWG) aims to demonstrate that an R-based submission can be successfully reviewed by the FDA.

By May 2024, the RCRSWG completed three pilot submissions and received response letters from the FDA's Center for Drug Evaluation and Research (CDER), marking a significant milestone. These submissions are the first publicly available packages that use open-source languages.

This presentation will share highlights from May 2024, where three pilot submissions from RCRSWG were completed successfully with response letters from the FDA's Center for Drug Evaluation and Research (CDER), marking a significant milestone. These submissions are the first publicly available packages that use open-source languages. 

Additionally, we will provide an update on the ongoing work in Pilot 4 as well as explore the potential of R in regulatory submissions and the RCRSWG's efforts to improve R-based clinical trial submissions.

All materials are available on the R Consortium's GitHub page, showcasing our commitment to transparency and innovation in regulatory submissions.

title: Responsive Regulation of AI in Drug Development
description:
Responsive Regulation of AI in Drug Development - Tala Fakhouri

Abstract: Over the past few decades, the volume of data available to support drug and biological product development have increased substantially. These increases in data volume were also accompanied by an expansion in data diversity with data originating from disparate sources including biologic data, pharmacological data, and clinical data (e.g., imaging, electronic health records, and digital health technologies). This growth in data volume and complexity combined with cutting-edge computing power and methodological advancements in artificial intelligence (AI) have the potential to transform how drugs and biological products are developed, manufactured, and utilized. Concurrent with these technological advancements, FDA’s Center for Drug Evaluation and Research (CDER) have seen an exponential increase in the number of drug submissions using AI components, with over 200 submissions reported in 2023 compared to only 1 in 2016. These submissions traverse the drug product life cycle from drug discovery to postmarket safety monitoring and cut across a range of therapeutic areas. In general, the application of AI in these submissions aims to improve the efficiency of drug discovery and our understanding of the efficacy and safety of specific treatments. The diverse uses of AI in these submissions highlight the need for an adaptive regulatory assessment of benefits and risks and emphasizes the importance of adopting a risk-based approach for establishing and evaluating the credibility of an AI model output for a particular context of use.

title: Harnessing CDISC's Emerging Analysis Results Datasets Standard
description:
Harnessing CDISC's Emerging Analysis Results Datasets Standard wtih the {cards} and {gtsummary} Packages - Daniel Sjoberg

Abstract: The CDISC Analysis Results Data (ARD) Model is an emerging standard for encoding statistical analysis summaries in a machine-readable format. Its primary objective is to streamline the processes of automation, ensuring reproducibility, promoting reusability, and enhancing traceability.

The newly released {cards} R package, a collaborative effort in the Pharmaverse including Roche, GSK, and Novartis, offers a variety of functions for ARD generation. These range from basic univariate summaries like means and tabulations to complex multivariable summaries encompassing regression models and statistical tests.

While {cards} calculates statistics and stores them in a structured object, it cannot present those results; this, however, is where the {gtsummary} R package shines. The {gtsummary} package offers a modular framework to construct summary tables. It won the American Statistical Association’s 2021 award for Innovation in Statistical Programming and Analytics, and also won first place in Posit BPC’s Table Contest’s pharmaceutical track. The {gtsummary} package was recently refactored to utilize {cards} as its backend, which will allow users to both extract an ARD object from a {gtsummary} table and use an ARD object to construct a {gtsummary} table. 

Our recent experience utilizing the {cards} and {gtsummary} R packages  to prepare a health authority filing highlighted the benefits of using ARDs, including improved automation, reproducibility, reusability, and traceability. We believe that this approach will become increasingly essential for ensuring the quality and efficiency of clinical trial reporting.

title: Sample Size or Power Calculations with Hierarchical Endpoints
description:
Sample Size or Power Calculations for Trial Design with Hierarchical Endpoints - An R Shiny App - Huiman Barnhart, Dylan Thibault

Abstract: Win measures, such as win ratio, win odds, net benefit, and desirability of outcome ranking (DOOR), have become popular approaches for the analysis of hierarchical endpoints in clinical studies. For trial design with these win measures based on hierarchical endpoints, sample size and power calculations are often based on simulation studies that can be cumbersome.  Existing sample size and power formulas require investigators to specify clinically significant and meaningful magnitude of win measures and probability of ties that are difficult to elicit based on prior published literature or preliminary data.  To overcome these difficulties, we express the win measures as function of marginal win measures that are readily available under independence assumption and this provides a novel way to specify a meaningful and justifiable magnitude of win measures and the magnitude of probability of ties. We built a very flexible R shiny app (https://duke-som.shinyapps.io/Hierarchical_Endpoints/) that can quickly compute a sample size with a given power or compute a power with a given sample size for any clinical trial design with two groups for any number of hierarchical endpoints, any type of endpoints (time to event, counts, binary, or ordinal), and any order of hierarchy of these endpoints.

Resources mentioned in the talk:

title: Optimizing Team Collaboration with Shiny
description:
Optimizing Team Collaboration with Shiny - Shannon Haughton

Abstract: In the fast moving Pharmaceutical industry, efficiency between teams is key. This was at the forefront of our minds when launching our shiny application GLITTER, built to take data input from our programming teams and produce formatted word documents for our medical writers.

However, when launching our application we experienced some teething problems. Our data inputs were not always fit for purpose, leading to time consuming communication loops between the two teams to bug fix and reach the desired outcome.

In this talk, I will discuss how through some small tweaks to our shiny application, we were able to produce a companion tool - GLITTER for programmers. This not only empowered our programmers to validate their own inputs, but also reduced unnecessary feedback cycles between teams. This showcases that the key to effective tooling often lies within existing code, it's just a matter of rethinking its application.

title: R Package Validation Framework: Docker Integration
description:
R Package Validation Framework: Docker Integration - Nicole Jones

Abstract: With the rapid adoption of R in the pharmaceutical industry, the topic of R package validation has become a focal point of discussion. Ensuring that these packages perform in a reproducible and reliable manner is not just important, but essential for maintaining the integrity of data analysis and regulatory compliance. Across the industry, numerous professionals have shared their methodologies for validating open-source R packages, each contributing valuable insights into best practices and common challenges. Despite the wealth of shared knowledge, one area remains relatively unexplored: the validation of R packages within Docker images. Docker, a platform that enables the creation and deployment of applications in isolated containers, offers a powerful tool for ensuring consistency across different computing environments. By encapsulating R packages and their dependencies within Docker images, we can achieve a high level of reproducibility and portability. This talk aims to delve into this niche yet significant topic and provide a guide to tailoring your validation process to leverage Docker for R package validation, enhancing the reproducibility and reliability of computational results in the pharmaceutical industry.

title: Apply machine learning methods to retinal clinical data in R
description:
Apply machine learning methods to retinal clinical data in R - Jin Ge

Abstract: Our objective was to develop predictive models for fibrosis, which is a crucial symptom response in the retinal health, using baseline characteristics and to identify key biomarkers associated with fibrosis progression. We utilized a comprehensive dataset that includes demographic information, ophthalmic exams (such as OCT and fluorescein angiography), and treatment details from the clinical trials.
The data preparation involved integrating multiple datasets to create a robust training set. We employed various machine learning techniques, including ensemble methods (XGBoost, Random Forest), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and partial least squares regression, to develop our models. The models' performance was evaluated based on sensitivity, specificity, and area under the ROC curve (AUC).

In the work, all results are done in R markdown which integrate the code, comments and result. Besides, data manipulation is fitted with clinical standard to display demographic statistics table. We hope the result from R could be used in clinics and also create a package for other users to run the machine learning methods as a standardized way in the future.

title: DuckDB and duckplyr: An in-process database management system in your R script
description:
DuckDB and duckplyr: An in-process database management system in your R script - Gabor Szarnyas

Abstract: DuckDB is an open-source analytical database management system with clients for several languages, including R. DuckDB offers the functionality of a database system, including high performance, persistence and full SQL support. At the same time, DuckDB has a small footprint with no dependency on an external server: it is trivial to deploy using the library(duckdb) call. DuckDB also has deep integrations into client libraries such as dplyr using the duckplyr package. In this talk, I explain how DuckDB achieves its high performance, demonstrate its performance through a live demo and showcase its R integrations.

title: Summer is Coming: AI for R, Shiny, and Pharma
description:
Summer is Coming: AI for R, Shiny, and Pharma - Joe Cheng

Abstract: R users tend to be skeptical of modern AI models, given our weird insistence on answers being accurate, or at least supported by the data. But I believe the time has come—or maybe it’s a little late—for even the most AI-cynical among us to push past their discomfort and get informed about what these tools are truly capable of. And key to that is moving beyond using AI-enabled apps, and towards building our own scripts, packages, and apps that make judicious use of AI.

In this talk, I’ll tell you why I believe AI has more to offer the R community than just wrong answers from chat windows or mediocre code suggestions in our IDEs. I’ll also introduce brand-new tools we’re developing at Posit that put powerful AI tools within reach of every R user. And finally, I’ll show how adding some AI could make your next Shiny app dramatically more useful for your users.

By adapting a grid search on a canonical model to use a more performant modeling engine, hooking into a parallel computing framework, transitioning to an optimized search strategy, and defining the grid to search over carefully, users can drastically cut down on the time to develop machine learning models with tidymodels without sacrificing predictive performance.

title: Harnessing the Power of tempusverse on the Tempus Lens Platform
description:
Accelerating Cancer Drug Discovery: Harnessing the Power of tempusverse on the Tempus Lens Platform - Nitesh Turaga

Abstract: The use of real-world evidence (RWE) has become increasingly crucial for drug discovery and development. However, researchers often face challenges in standardizing clinical and molecular approaches to efficiently analyze and interpret vast amounts of data from multiple sources. To transform the analytical process, we developed tempusverse, a suite of R packages to analyze Tempus and public datasets on the Tempus Lens Workspaces platform.

The tempusverse is a comprehensive collection of production-ready R packages designed with modularity, reproducibility, and robust versioning in mind. This thoughtful design not only streamlines the analysis of one of the world's largest real-world cancer databases but also ensures research integrity is preserved throughout the analytical process. The packages offer standardized methodology for data wrangling, analysis, and visualization, enabling researchers to gain fast insights and make informed decisions rapidly.

By combining the power of tempusverse with the production-grade workspace environment provided by the Tempus Lens platform, researchers can seamlessly integrate public data sources, efficiently process information, and generate publication-ready visualizations. The symbiotic relationship between tempusverse and Lens empowers pharmaceutical researchers to accelerate the discovery of novel cancer therapeutics while maintaining the highest standards of scientific rigor.
In this session, we will demonstrate how this innovative approach enhances decision-making, increases the probability of technical success in clinical trials, and ultimately improves patient outcomes. Join us to explore how the tempusverse, coupled with the Lens platform, is revolutionizing the way we leverage real-world evidence in cancer drug discovery, all while ensuring the reproducibility and integrity of research findings.

Resources mentioned in the talk:
- Tempus: AI-enabled precision medicine https://www.tempus.com

title: A Novel Bayesian Evaluation of Benefits and Risks for Medical Products -
description:
A Novel yet Practical Bayesian Evaluation of Benefits and Risks for Medical Products with a Companion R-Shiny Application - Sarabh Mukhopadhyay

Abstract: Evaluation of the benefit-risk profile is a key element throughout the life cycle of a medical product. There is an increasing emphasis on quantitative benefit-risk assessments, as it aims to define explicit, transparent, and evidence-based criteria to compare benefit-risk profiles of different treatments that can inform sponsors, regulators, payers, and other stakeholders at different stages of its entire life cycle. One challenge in implementing quantitative benefit risk is the elicitation of utility functions to map observed outcomes to numeric scores. In this presentation, we first discuss a Bayesian framework that provides an explicit and efficient way to estimate preferences using trade-off data from stakeholders. A key gap remains in how to effectively combine preferences elicited with data from clinical trials. Therefore, we next characterize the uncertainty of the performance of a treatment based on clinical trial data. Finally, we discuss how to combine preferences with outcomes of treatment to define an overall benefit-risk utility score of a treatment. The methodology also allows stochastic comparisons between two or more treatments or doses to understand relative preferences and associated uncertainties. This comprehensive approach is also easy to implement in practice and can leverage aggregate-level clinical trial data to estimate treatment performance. We demonstrate the entire process using simulated data and an R-shiny app.

Resources mentioned in the talk:

title: Code that Cures: Validating GxP-Compliant R Shiny Apps for Clinical Decisions
description:
Code that Cures: Validating GxP-Compliant R Shiny Apps for Clinical Decisions - Emily Yates

Abstract: GxP-relevant R Shiny application validation ensures compliance and data integrity in regulated industries. This complex process relies on three key pillars: people, technology, and processes. A cross-functional team is crucial, comprising an R champion to drive initiatives, technical experts to implement infrastructure, and QA partners to guide regulatory adherence. Essential technologies include validated infrastructure and packages, forming the foundation for reliable application development. Processes should align with GAMP 5 guidelines and incorporate risk-based Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) principles, such as documented user requirements and robust code testing. This overview serves as a starting point for those interested in building validated R Shiny applications, highlighting the importance of collaboration, risk-based approaches, and continuous learning. By following these guidelines and leveraging available resources, organizations can successfully implement and maintain validated R Shiny applications in GxP environments, ensuring regulatory compliance and operational efficiency.

title: Using R to Return Clinical Trial Data to Participants
description:
Using R to Return Clinical Trial Data to Participants - Sam Parmar

Abstract: Pfizer has done a lot in the last several years to cultivate a positive journey for clinical trial participants. However, returning personal trial data to patients has always been the missing piece, until now. In August 2023, Pfizer launched the Clinical Trial Participant Data Return Initiative. U.S. participants from Pfizer sponsored studies are now able to register on the Pfizer Clinical Trial Alumni platform to receive their data. The participant’s data report includes the participant’s full trial record. The reports are powered by R Markdown and soon Quarto. In this talk, I’d like to share the story of how open-source tools from Posit have enabled this effort to provide PDF data reports to clinical trial participants.

title: Building pathways for Inclusive life sciences
description:
Building pathways for Inclusive life sciences and tech careers : Learnings from useR!2024 panel - Audrey Yeo, Daphne Grasselly, Magdelena Krochmal

Abstract: In the year 2024, many changes within the R Pharma community have triggered a focus on improving diversity, equity and inclusion. The Pharmaverse council for example, composed of a lack of heterogeneity had started conversations and actions to improve gender diversity (council, pharmaverse. 2024. “Diversity & Inclusion in Pharmaverse.” May 31, 202.). At the useR!2024 conference held in Salzburg, Austria between July 8-11, three Roche employees joined forces to present a panel discussion entitled “Inclusive Pathways in Life Sciences and Tech”. This initiative was first conceived in a discussion with members of R Ladies, R Forwards, Linux Foundation, the diversity council consisting of other pharma employees and many more were consulted on the scope, title and purpose. A survey was also started and data was presented at the panel discussion. With great consideration of impact on an international audience of future and current package developers, the panel additionally demonstrated business and ethical cases for a more diverse workforce in life science and technology. Furthermore, the panel acknowledge the awareness of implicit bias and other systematic structures that currently hinder a diverse workforce as “half the battle”. The panel discussion stressed the importance for individual contributors and managers to take a life-long learning approach with small and concrete steps as a start. Building on the growing influence of the R language in life sciences and tech careers, this talk will summarize the efforts of the panel and discussion started at the useR!2024 conference to the R/Pharma audience.

title: Integrating GenAI with Open
description: Over the past 7 years, NEST packages have been established as a default toolkit for generating clinical insights at Roche. Starting this year, all the new clinical trials will adopt NEST tools by default for their reporting events. To reduce change management burden and boost data scientist’s productivity and efficiency at work, we started leveraging the latest GenAI technologies and built a chatbot that could help beginners to learn about the new open-source R packages introduced to their daily work. In this talk, we will share our journey so far of NExpert, an in-house GenAI-enabled chatbot for NEST and other open-source toolkits, and how we envision GenAI/LLM could be embedded in the daily work of data scientists in our industry.
author: Source for Insights Generation to Boost Efficiency

title: Digesting the Landscape of LLMs & Successfully Adopting them in Regulatory Contexts
description: Devin Pastoor shares a comprehensive overview of the current landscape of Large Language Models (LLMs) and details key concepts that underpin their strengths and limitations, as well as sharing the components that make up solutions built around LLMs, and highlights tips for success to adopt LLMs in an organization.
author: Devin Pastoor

title: Supporting the Medical Writing Process with R, Shiny, and GenAI
description:
Medical writers play a critical role in the development of clinical study report (CSR) documents by synthesizing and organizing data from clinical trials. They collaborate closely with researchers, statisticians, and other experts to ensure that the CSR accurately and comprehensively presents the study findings in adherence to regulatory guidelines. This involves incorporating the study protocol, statistical analyses, safety and efficacy results, and other pertinent information. Medical writers also prioritize clarity, conciseness, and adherence to the highest standards of scientific and medical accuracy in the CSR.

In the past, the medical writing process was largely manual. Previous attempts to automate aspects of the writing process relied on rule-based approaches, which were precise but inherently time-consuming to maintain, extend, and adjust. However, recent advancements in Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) and Large Language Models (LLMs) have significantly improved the potential for generating text from data.

We have developed an end-to-end Shiny application to support Medical Writers in drafting CSRs. This application provides secure access to clinical data tables, interactive configuration and filtering of data tables, and the generation and adjustment of scientific texts using LLMs. It also facilitates the storage of generated content in document templates which include Quarto reports. The application is currently in productive use, demonstrating robustness and modularization, making it easily extendable.
author: Robert Adams & Matthew Kumar

title: Rapid Absorption of Dataset Knowledge through GPT
description: This talk addresses the challenges in effectively leveraging the full capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) for data extraction: creating effective knowledge corpora and learning workflow options. We'll explore strategies for handling diverse file formats including PDFs, CSVs, Excel sheets, and unstructured text, as well as data volume limitations. The presentation will demonstrate practical workflows for loading, transforming, and extracting data using GPT-enhanced processes, with a focus on optimizing import/export operations across different formats. We'll discuss techniques for creating and curating knowledge corpora  and developing custom GPTs tailored to specific document types and industry needs. Real-world examples from pharmaceutical and clinical research will illustrate how these techniques can significantly accelerate document processing, improve data accessibility, and unlock insights, showcasing the transformative potential of GPT-enhanced documentation in rapidly absorbing and synthesizing information.
author: Enhanced Documentation

title: Modelling Regulatory Intelligence with GenAI
description: This research introduces an innovative GenAI approach to enhance regulatory compliance in the pharmaceutical industry. Faced with evolving FDA and EMA regulations, pharmaceutical companies struggle with manual, error-prone processes for updating internal documents. Our study proposes a two-step AI-driven solution to streamline regulatory update management. Step 1 employs semantic search to identify potentially impacted Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) when new regulations are introduced. Step 2 utilizes a ReAct (Reasoning and Acting) agent powered by the GPT-4o model to perform detailed comparisons between regulations and each identified SOP, confirming impacts and pinpointing specific areas requiring updates. This automated system aims to reduce manual workload, minimize oversight risks, and ensure timely alignment with regulatory changes. The study demonstrates the potential of GenAI in transforming regulatory intelligence, offering a scalable solution to a critical industry challenge. Future work will focus on model refinement and real-world implementation to quantify benefits in time-saving and compliance accuracy.
author: Jake Gagnon

title: Leveraging ChatGPT in Statistical Programming in the Pharmaceutical Industry
description: This presentation explores the potential benefits of incorporating ChatGPT, a state-of-the-art natural language processing model, in statistical programming within the pharmaceutical industry. By leveraging ChatGPT's capabilities, this technology can save time, money, and most importantly, your sanity. Programming often leads to frustration, anxiety, and sleepless nights trying to solve complex problems. Various applications and techniques that harness the power of ChatGPT will be described to reduce all of these. In a world where artificial intelligence threatens to take our jobs, this paper suggests methods of tapping into the untapped potential of ChatGPT to empower programmers with innovative tools, thereby increasing their value. When programming issues arise, no longer will you need to worry about judgement or hostility from others on online forums, particularly the wrath experienced when not including a reproducible example. ChatGPT is a powerful tool we have yet to fully leverage, and its benefits extend well beyond our imaginations, let alone this presentation.
